main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Continual pre-training with increased number of epochs on a subset of the corpus instead of one epoch on the full corpus","Data sampling strategy selecting highest-quality tokens for domain knowledge","Data mixture strategy matching pre-training data distribution to reduce distribution shift and knowledge forgetting"],"problems":["Initial performance drop (stability gap) during domain continual pre-training of LLMs","Inefficiency and instability in continual pre-training due to distribution shift and training settings","Knowledge forgetting of general instruction-following ability during domain adaptation"],"datasets":["Medical domain corpus (for continual pre-training)","Legal domain corpus (for continual pre-training)","General instruction-following benchmarks","Medical task benchmarks (unspecified, but used for Llama-3-Physician and OpenLlama-3B evaluation)"],"metrics":["Average medical task performance (%)","Average general task performance (%)","Ratio of model’s task performance to best performance among all models"],"results":[{"metric":"Average medical task performance (OpenLlama-3B, before strategies)","value":"36.2%"},{"metric":"Average medical task performance (OpenLlama-3B, after strategies, 40% training budget)","value":"40.7%"},{"metric":"Llama-3-Physician medical performance","value":"Best among current open-source models; comparable to or better than GPT-4 on several medical benchmarks"}],"novelty_claims":["First to identify and systematically analyze the stability gap (initial performance drop) in LLM domain continual pre-training across domains and model sizes","Introduce the stability gap concept from vision models to explain LLM behavior during continual pre-training","Propose three efficient strategies (multi-epoch subset training, high-quality token selection, and data mixture matching) to mitigate the stability gap and improve continual pre-training efficiency and effectiveness","Demonstrate that these strategies can achieve higher performance with reduced compute budget and without causing forgetting of general abilities"]}

methods: Continual pre-training with increased number of epochs on a subset of the corpus instead of one epoch on the full corpus
Data sampling strategy selecting highest-quality tokens for domain knowledge
Data mixture strategy matching pre-training data distribution to reduce distribution shift and knowledge forgetting
problems: Initial performance drop (stability gap) during domain continual pre-training of LLMs
Inefficiency and instability in continual pre-training due to distribution shift and training settings
Knowledge forgetting of general instruction-following ability during domain adaptation
datasets: Medical task benchmarks (unspecified, but used for Llama-3-Physician and OpenLlama-3B evaluation)
metrics: Ratio of model’s task performance to best performance among all models

results.metric: Average medical task performance (OpenLlama-3B, before strategies)
Average medical task performance (OpenLlama-3B, after strategies, 40% training budget)

results.value: Best among current open-source models; comparable to or better than GPT-4 on several medical benchmarks

novelty_claims: First to identify and systematically analyze the stability gap (initial performance drop) in LLM domain continual pre-training across domains and model sizes
Introduce the stability gap concept from vision models to explain LLM behavior during continual pre-training

Propose three efficient strategies (multi-epoch subset training, high-quality token selection, and data mixture matching) to mitigate the stability gap and improve continual pre-training efficiency and effectiveness
Demonstrate that these strategies can achieve higher performance with reduced compute budget and without causing forgetting of general abilities

methods: Continual pre-training on a subset of the corpus for multiple epochs instead of a large corpus for one epoch

novelty_claims: First to systematically study and mitigate the stability gap in continual pre-training of LLMs for domain adaptation, especially in the medical domain

Demonstrate that their strategies can achieve higher performance with less compute budget and without knowledge forgetting
Release of Llama-3-Physician, a model that achieves state-of-the-art medical performance among open-source LLMs and is competitive with GPT-4

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Semi-automated pipeline for generating a diverse medical instruction-finetuning (IFT) dataset (MedInstruct-52k) using GPT-4 and ChatGPT, seeded with expert-curated tasks","Instruction-finetuning of LLaMA-series models on the MedInstruct-52k dataset to create AlpaCare","Creation of a new clinician-crafted free-form instruction evaluation test set (MedInstruct-test)"],"problems":["Limited diversity in existing biomedical instruction-finetuning datasets for LLMs, leading to poor generalizability and instruction-following ability in medical applications","High cost and privacy concerns in collecting real-world medical instruction datasets","Lack of effective methods to generate diverse, domain-specific IFT datasets for medical LLM alignment"],"datasets":["MedInstruct-52k (52,000 machine-generated medical instruction-response pairs)","MedInstruct-test (216 clinician-crafted medical tasks)","Multiple general domain benchmarks (not named specifically in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Medical free-form instruction evaluation accuracy/gain","General domain benchmark average gain","Human evaluation: correctness","Human evaluation: helpfulness"],"results":[{"metric":"Medical free-form instruction evaluation (absolute gain over best baselines)","value":"38.1%"},{"metric":"General domain benchmarks (average absolute gain)","value":"6.7%"},{"metric":"Human evaluation - methods: Learning rate (LR) re-warming and re-decaying during continual pre-training
Replay of previous data during continual pre-training
Infinite learning rate schedules for smooth transition across datasets
problems: Efficiently updating large language models (LLMs) with new data without full re-training
Mitigating performance degradation due to distribution shift in continual pre-training
Avoiding catastrophic forgetting of previous knowledge during continual pre-training

metrics: Average evaluation performance (on language model evaluation benchmarks)
novelty_claims: Demonstrates that a simple and scalable combination of LR re-warming, LR re-decaying, and replay can match the performance of full re-training from scratch on all available data, even under significant distribution shift and at large model scales.
Shows that continual pre-training with these strategies can achieve similar performance to re-training while using only a fraction of the compute.
Proposes infinite learning rate schedules as a new approach to circumvent forgetting and optimization difficulties in continual pre-training, not bound to a fixed token budget.
Provides large-scale empirical evidence (up to 10B parameter models and hundreds of billions of tokens) for the effectiveness of these continual learning strategies.

Efficiently adapting models pretrained on short contexts (e.g., 4K) to much longer contexts without prohibitive computational cost
Avoiding performance degradation in short-context tasks when scaling to long contexts
datasets: Pretraining corpora with balanced domain mixtures (specific datasets not named, but includes books and other domains)
novelty_claims: Demonstrates that only 1-5B tokens of continual pretraining on long-context data are sufficient to scale context length to 128K, as opposed to prior works using 400B tokens.
Shows that maintaining domain balance in the data mixture, rather than naively upsampling long-sequence domains, is crucial for stable long- and short-context performance.
Closes the performance gap to frontier models like GPT-4 128K on the Needle-in-a-Haystack test using open-source models and academic-level resources.
Proposes a concrete, affordable data recipe for context scaling that is effective for both 7B and 13B LLaMA-2 models.
